paragraphs.label: A
paragraphs.text: The kākāpō is a nocturnal, flightless parrot that is critically endangered and one of New Zealand’s unique treasures

paragraphs.label: B
paragraphs.text: The kākāpō, also known as the owl parrot, is a large, forest-dwelling bird, with a pale owl-like face. Up to 64 cm in length, it has predominantly yellow-green feathers, forward-facing eyes, a large grey beak, large blue feet, and relatively short wings and tail. It is the world’s only flightless parrot, and is also possibly one of the world’s longest-living birds, with a reported lifespan of up to 100 years.

paragraphs.label: C
paragraphs.text: Kākāpō are solitary birds and tend to occupy the same home range for many years. They forage on the ground and climb high into trees. They often leap from trees and flap their wings, but at best manage a controlled descent to the ground. They are entirely vegetarian, with their diet including the leaves, roots and bark of trees as well as bulbs, and fern fronds.

paragraphs.label: D
paragraphs.text: Kākāpō breed in summer and autumn, but only in years when food is plentiful. Males play no part in incubation or chick-rearing – females alone incubate eggs and feed the chicks. The 1–4 eggs are laid in soil, which is repeatedly turned over before and during incubation. The female kākāpō has to spend long periods away from the nest searching for food, which leaves the unattended eggs and chicks particularly vulnerable to predators.

paragraphs.label: E
paragraphs.text: Before humans arrived, kākāpō were common throughout New Zealand’s forests. However, this all changed with the arrival of the first Polynesian settlers about 700 years ago. For the early settlers, the flightless kākāpō was easy prey. They ate its meat and used its feathers to make soft cloaks. With them came the Polynesian dog and rat, which also preyed on kākāpō. By the time European colonisers arrived in the early 1800s, kākāpō had become confined to the central North Island and forested parts of the South Island. The fall in kākāpō numbers was accelerated by European colonisation. A great deal of habitat was lost through forest clearance, and introduced species such as deer depleted the remaining forests of food. Other predators such as cats, stoats and two more species of rat were also introduced. The kākāpō were in serious trouble.

paragraphs.label: F
paragraphs.text: In 1894, the New Zealand government launched its first attempt to save the kākāpō. Conservationist Richard Henry led an effort to relocate several hundred of the birds to predator-free Resolution Island in Fiordland. Unfortunately, the island didn’t remain predator free – stoats arrived within six years, eventually destroying the kākāpō population. By the mid-1900s, the kākāpō was practically a lost species. Only a few clung to life in the most isolated parts of New Zealand.

paragraphs.label: G
paragraphs.text: From 1949 to 1973, the newly formed New Zealand Wildlife Service made over 60 expeditions to find kākāpō, focusing mainly on Fiordland. Six were caught, but there were no females amongst them and all but one died within a few months of captivity. In 1974, a new initiative was launched, and by 1977, 18 more kākāpō were found in Fiordland. However, there were still no females. In 1977, a large population of males was spotted in Rakiura – a large island free from stoats, ferrets and weasels. There were about 200 individuals, and in 1980 it was confirmed females were also present. These birds have been the foundation of all subsequent work in managing the species.

paragraphs.label: H
paragraphs.text: Unfortunately, predation by feral cats on Rakiura Island led to a rapid decline in kākāpō numbers. As a result, during 1980–97, the surviving population was evacuated to three island sanctuaries: Codfish Island, Maud Island and Little Barrier Island. However, breeding success was hard to achieve. Rats were found to be a major predator of kākāpō chicks and an insufficient number of chicks survived to offset adult mortality. By 1995, although at least 12 chicks had been produced on the islands, only three had survived. The kākāpō population had dropped to 51 birds. The critical situation prompted an urgent review of kākāpō management in New Zealand.

paragraphs.label: I
paragraphs.text:
In 1996, a new Recovery Plan was launched, together with a specialist advisory group called the Kākāpō Scientific and Technical Advisory Committee and a higher amount of funding. Renewed steps were taken to control predators on the three islands. Cats were eradicated from Little Barrier Island in 1980, and possums were eradicated from Codfish Island by 1986. However, the population did not start to increase until rats were removed from all three islands, and the birds were more intensively managed. 
This involved moving the birds between islands, supplementary feeding of adults and rescuing and hand-raising any failing chicks.

paragraphs.label: J
paragraphs.text: After the first five years of the Recovery Plan, the population was on target. By 2000, five new females had been produced, and the total population had grown to 62 birds. For the first time, there was cautious optimism for the future of kākāpō and by June 2020, a total of 210 birds was recorded.

paragraphs.label: K
paragraphs.text: Today, kākāpō management continues to be guided by the kākāpō Recovery Plan. Its key goals are: minimise the loss of genetic diversity in the kākāpō population, restore or maintain sufficient habitat to accommodate the expected increase in the kākāpō population, and ensure stakeholders continue to be fully engaged in the preservation of the species.

 第二步：寻找答案句。通过扫描原文，在第二段发现关键句：“It is the world’s only flightless parrot...”，其中“flightless”直接对应题干的“inability to fly”，“the world’s only”则涉及“是否有其他鹦鹉”的核心问题。
 第三步：答案句理解。该句明确指出kakapo是“世界上唯一不会飞的鹦鹉”，“only”（唯一的）表明不存在其他具有相同“不会飞”特征的鹦鹉，直接否定了题干中“other parrots”（其他鹦鹉）的说法。

questions.title: The Polynesian rat was a greater threat to the kakapo than Polynesian settlers.
questions.analysis:
第一步：定位题目关键词。题干核心信息为“Polynesian rat”“Polynesian settlers”“threat”“kakapo”以及比较关系“a greater... than”。通过这些关键词，在原文中定位到第四段关于波利尼西亚定居者和其带来的动物对鸮鹦鹉影响的内容。
 第二步：寻找答案句。原文第四段明确提到波利尼西亚定居者（Polynesian settlers）将鸮鹦鹉（kākāpō）作为猎物（prey），吃其肉、用其羽毛；同时提到随定居者而来的波利尼西亚鼠（Polynesian rat）也捕食（preyed on）鸮鹦鹉。这部分内容是与题干最相关的答案句。
 第三步：分析答案句内容。答案句仅客观陈述了两个事实：①波利尼西亚定居者捕食鸮鹦鹉；②波利尼西亚鼠也捕食鸮鹦鹉。但原文中没有任何信息对“波利尼西亚鼠的威胁”和“波利尼西亚定居者的威胁”的程度进行比较，即未提及“谁的威胁更大”。

questions.title: Kakapo were transferred from Rakiura Island to other locations because they were at risk from feral cats.
questions.analysis:
1. **题目定位**：题干核心信息为“Kakapo从Rakiura Island转移到其他地方的原因是受野猫威胁”，需在原文中寻找“转移行为”“转移地点”“转移原因”及“野猫”相关内容。
 2. **关键词匹配**：通过题干中的“Kakapo”“transferred”“Rakiura Island”“feral cats”等关键词，在原文中定位到关键段落，其中“kākāpō”“surviving population”对应“Kakapo”，“was evacuated to”对应“were transferred”，“on Rakiura Island”对应“from Rakiura Island”，“three island sanctuaries...”对应“other locations”，“predation by feral cats...led to a rapid decline”对应“at risk from feral cats”，“As a result”对应“because”。
 3. **答案句理解**：定位句明确阐述了因果关系——“野猫捕食导致鸮鹦鹉数量下降（原因）”，“因此幸存种群被转移到其他岛屿（结果）”，与题干“因受野猫威胁而转移”的逻辑完全一致。

questions.title: One Recovery Plan initiative that helped increase the kakapo population size was caring for struggling young birds.
questions.analysis:
第一步：题目定位。题干核心信息为“Recovery Plan的一项举措帮助增加kākāpō种群数量，该举措是照料处境艰难的幼鸟”。通过关键词“Recovery Plan”“increase population”“caring for young birds”，可定位到文章倒数第二段（讲述1996年启动新恢复计划及后续效果的段落）。
 第二步：寻找答案句。在该段落中，先提到“the population did not start to increase until... the birds were more intensively managed”，随后用“This involved...”具体说明“更集中的管理”措施，其中包含“rescuing and hand-raising any failing chicks”（拯救并人工喂养发育不良的雏鸟）。接着明确指出结果：“After the first five years of the Recovery Plan... the total population had grown to 62 birds”（恢复计划头五年后，总种群数量增长到62只）。这些句子构成了完整的答案句。
 第三步：答案句理解。答案句表明，“恢复计划”中的“拯救并人工喂养发育不良的雏鸟”是具体举措之一，且该举措实施后种群数量确实增长，与题干“照料处境艰难的幼鸟帮助增加种群数量”的表述完全一致。

central_sentences.sentence: This involved moving the birds between islands, supplementary feeding of adults and rescuing and hand-raising any failing chicks. After the first five years of the Recovery Plan, the population was on target. By 2000, five new females had been produced, and the total population had grown to 62 birds.
central_sentences.translation: 这包括在岛屿之间转移鸟类、对成年鸟进行补充喂养以及拯救并人工喂养任何发育不良的雏鸟。在恢复计划实施的头五年后，种群数量达到了目标。到2000年，已培育出5只新的雌性个体，总种群数量增长到62只。

content.title: Return of the elm: reintroducing the beloved tree to Britain
content.summary: {'A': '20世纪60 - 70年代，英国约2500万棵榆树因荷兰榆树病死亡，榆树在英国景观中曾占主导地位，如今虽被淡忘，但有望重新引入英格兰中部和南部乡村，不过起点很低。', 'B': '荷兰榆树病由一种真菌引起，它会阻塞榆树维管系统，20世纪20年代首次流行后渐弱，70年代因从加拿大运来用于造船的榆木触发第二次流行，携带更具毒性菌株的甲虫摧毁了绝大多数英国榆树。', 'C': '如今英格兰南部乡村仍有榆树，但多在田间矮树篱中，树干直径达10 - 15厘米易受甲虫侵害，不过在一些郡发现了数百岁的成熟榆树，关键是研究这些幸存者为何能抵御疫情，但机会有限。', 'D': '几个世纪以来，榆树在英国是仅次于橡木的硬木首选，在欧洲森林常见，其使用可追溯到青铜时代，用于工具、盾牌等，18世纪种植更广，用于储物箱、地板等。', 'E': '榆树在英国文化中根深蒂固，有很多拥护者，如布莱顿国家榆树收藏馆的彼得·伯恩，布莱顿约有1.7万棵榆树，海风使甲虫难以攻击，但情况仍不稳定。', 'F': '榆树重新引入很大程度依赖其对疾病的抗性或耐受性，广泛重新引入可能涉及现有或新的杂交品种，已培育并测试新一代幼苗对真菌的抵抗力。', 'G': '重新引入榆树存在社会接受度问题，有人认为环境方面有理由重新引入，也有人担心用园艺类似物替代本土物种、克隆及引入新疾病的风险，林地信托强调为自然存活的榆树提供空间。'}

paragraphs.label: A
paragraphs.text: Around 25 million elms, accounting for 90% of all elm trees in the UK, died during the 1960s and '70s from Dutch elm disease. In the aftermath, the elm, once so dominant in the British landscape, was largely forgotten. However, there's now hope the elm may be reintroduced to the countryside of central and southern England. Any reintroduction will start from a very low base. 'The impact of the disease is difficult to picture if you hadn't seen what was there before,' says Matt Elliot of the Woodland Trust. 'You look at old photographs from the 1960s and it's only then that you realise the impact [elms had] ... They were significant, large trees ... then they were gone.'

paragraphs.label: B
paragraphs.text: The disease is caused by a fungus that blocks the elms' vascular (water, nutrient and food transport) system, causing branches to wilt and die. A first epidemic, which occurred in the 1920s, gradually died down, but in the '70s a second epidemic was triggered by shipments of elm from Canada. The wood came in the form of logs destined for boat building and its intact bark was perfect for the elm bark beetles that spread the deadly fungus. This time, the beetles carried a much more virulent strain that destroyed the vast majority of British elms.

paragraphs.label: C
paragraphs.text:
Today, elms still exist in the southern English countryside but mostly only in low hedgerows between fields. 'We have millions of small elms in hedgerows but they get targeted by the beetle as soon as they reach a certain size,' says Karen Russell, co-author of the report 'Where we are with elm'. Once the trunk of the elm reaches 10–15 centimetres or so in diameter, it becomes a perfect size for beetles to lay eggs and for the fungus to take hold. Yet mature specimens have been identified, in counties such as Cambridgeshire, that are hundreds of years old, and have mysteriously escaped the epidemic.

The key, Russell says, is to identify and study those trees that have survived and work out why they stood tall when millions of others succumbed. Nevertheless, opportunities are limited as the number of these mature survivors is relatively small. 'What are the reasons for their survival?' asks Russell. 'Avoidance, tolerance, resistance? We don't know where the balance lies between the three. I don't see how it can be entirely down to luck.'

paragraphs.label: D
paragraphs.text: For centuries, elm ran a close second to oak as the hardwood tree of choice in Britain and was in many instances the most prominent tree in the landscape. Not only was elm common in European forests, it became a key component of birch, ash and hazel woodlands. The use of elm is thought to go back to the Bronze Age, when it was widely used for tools. Elm was also the preferred material for shields and early swords. In the 18th century, it was planted more widely and its wood was used for items such as storage crates and flooring. It was also suitable for items that experienced high levels of impact and was used to build the keel of the 19th-century sailing ship Cutty Sark as well as mining equipment.

paragraphs.label: E
paragraphs.text: Given how ingrained elm is in British culture, it's unsurprising the tree has many advocates. Amongst them is Peter Bourne of the National Elm Collection in Brighton. 'I saw Dutch elm disease unfold as a small boy,' he says. 'The elm seemed to be part of rural England, but I remember watching trees just lose their leaves and that really stayed with me.' Today, the city of Brighton's elms total about 17,000. Local factors appear to have contributed to their survival. Strong winds from the sea make it difficult for the determined elm bark beetle to attack this coastal city's elm population. However, the situation is precarious. 'The beetles can just march in if we're not careful, as the threat is right on our doorstep,' says Bourne.

paragraphs.label: F
paragraphs.text: Any prospect of the elm returning relies heavily on trees being either resistant to, or tolerant of, the disease. This means a widespread reintroduction would involve existing or new hybrid strains derived from resistant, generally non-native elm species. A new generation of seedlings have been bred and tested to see if they can withstand the fungus by cutting a small slit in the bark and injecting a tiny amount of the pathogen. 'The effects are very quick,' says Russell. 'You return in four to six weeks and trees that are resistant show no symptoms, whereas those that are susceptible show leaf loss and may even have died completely.'

paragraphs.label: G
paragraphs.text:
All of this raises questions of social acceptance, acknowledges Russell. 'If we're putting elm back into the landscape, a small element of it is not native – are we bothered about that?' For her, the environmental case for reintroducing elm is strong. 'They will host wildlife, which is a good thing.' Others are more wary. 'On the face of it, it seems like a good idea,' says Elliot. The problem, he suggests, is that, 'You're replacing a native species with a horticultural analogue*. You're effectively cloning.' There's also the risk of introducing new diseases. Rather than plant new elms, the Woodland Trust emphasises providing space to those elms that have survived independently. 'Sometimes the best thing you can do is just give nature time to recover ... over time, you might get resistance,' says Elliot.

* horticultural analogue: a cultivated plant species that is genetically similar to an existing species

questions.title: reference to the research problems that arise from there being only a few surviving large elms
questions.analysis:
1. 题目定位与关键词分析：题目核心是“reference to the research problems that arise from there being only a few surviving large elms”，即要找到提及“因现存大型榆树稀少而导致研究难题”的段落。需要拆解出“research problems”（研究难题）、“arise from”（由…引起）、“only a few”（数量少）、“surviving”（幸存的）、“large elms”（大型榆树）这些关键信息点。
 2. 在原文中寻找答案句：带着这些关键词浏览各段，在Section C中发现关键句：“The key, Russell says, is to identify and study those trees that have survived and work out why they stood tall when millions of others succumbed. Nevertheless, opportunities are limited as the number of these mature survivors is relatively small.” 这句话明确提到了研究目标（识别和研究幸存树木）以及研究面临的限制（机会有限）。
 3. 答案句理解与逻辑匹配：
 - “identify and study those trees that have survived”对应了“research”（研究）。
 - “opportunities are limited”直接点明了“research problems”（研究难题）。
 - “as”解释了原因，即“the number of these mature survivors is relatively small”，其中“as”对应“arise from”，“relatively small”对应“only a few”，“mature survivors”对应“surviving large elms”（因为前文提到小榆树易受攻击，能幸存的成熟榆树必然是较大的）。
 综上，Section C的这句话完整地包含了题干所要求的全部信息点，逻辑关系也完全匹配。

questions.title: reference to how Dutch elm disease was brought into Britain
questions.analysis:
1. **题目定位**：题干要求找到“提及荷兰榆树病如何被带入英国”的段落。核心在于“如何被带入”，即需定位到描述疾病传入方式或途径的内容。
 2. **关键词寻找**：拆解题干线索词，“reference to”对应原文“was triggered by”（提及原因/方式）；“how”对应“by shipments of elm from Canada”（具体传入方式）；“Dutch elm disease”对应“a second epidemic”（B段上下文明确“the disease”即荷兰榆树病，“second epidemic”为其第二次爆发）；“was brought into”对应“shipments ... from Canada”（运输行为体现“带入”）；“Britain”对应“British elms”（B段结尾“destroyed the vast majority of British elms”明确地点为英国）。
 3. **答案句理解**：答案句明确指出70年代的第二次荷兰榆树病疫情“由来自加拿大的榆木运输引发”，并补充说明木材为原木，树皮为传播真菌的甲虫提供生存环境，完整解释了疾病“被带入英国”的具体过程，直接回答题干问题。

questions.analysis:

 2. **定位段落**：快速浏览各段，A-G段中仅E段提到具体英国地点“Brighton”，并讨论其榆树“survival”（幸存），初步锁定E段。

questions.title: reference to the stage at which young elms become vulnerable to Dutch elm disease
questions.analysis:
1. **题目定位关键词**：题干核心信息为“the stage at which young elms become vulnerable to Dutch elm disease”，需重点定位“阶段（stage）”“幼年榆树（young elms）”“易感染（become vulnerable to）”“荷兰榆树病（Dutch elm disease）”这些关键概念。
 2. **寻找答案句**：在各段落中扫描与上述关键词相关的内容。Section C开头提到“We have millions of small elms in hedgerows”，对应“young elms”；随后明确指出“Once the trunk of the elm reaches 10–15 centimetres or so in diameter, it becomes a perfect size for beetles to lay eggs and for the fungus to take hold”，其中“Once...reaches...diameter”对应“the stage”，“becomes a perfect size for... the fungus to take hold”对应“become vulnerable to Dutch elm disease”（因荷兰榆树病由真菌引起，“the fungus”即指该病病原体）。
 3. **答案句理解**：该句清晰说明幼年榆树（小榆树）在树干直径达到10-15厘米左右时，会成为甲虫产卵和真菌扎根的目标，即进入易感染荷兰榆树病的阶段，直接回答了题干问题。

questions.title: If a tree gets infected with Dutch elm disease, the damage rapidly becomes visible.
questions.analysis:
第一步：题目定位。题干核心是“树木感染荷兰榆树病后，损害会迅速显现”，需要找到提到“感染后损害显现速度”的研究人员。
 第二步：关键词拆解与原文匹配。拆解题干关键词：“If a tree gets infected”（树木被感染）对应原文“trees that are susceptible”（易受感染的树）；“damage”（损害）对应“leaf loss”（落叶）“died completely”（完全死亡）；“rapidly”（迅速地）对应“very quick”（非常快）“in four to six weeks”（四到六周内）；“becomes visible”（变得可见）对应“show leaf loss”（表现出落叶）“may even have died completely”（完全死亡）。
 第三步：寻找答案句。在文章Section F中，Karen Russell提到测试树木抗病性时，给树木注射病原体后，“The effects are very quick”，且“四到六周内易受感染的树会落叶甚至死亡”，直接说明感染后损害“迅速显现”，因此答案句在此处。
 第四步：确定人物。该观点由“says Russell”引出，即Karen Russell，故匹配选项B。

central_sentences.sentence: 'The effects are very quick,' says Russell. 'You return in four to six weeks and trees that are resistant show no symptoms, whereas those that are susceptible show leaf loss and may even have died completely.'

questions.title: It may be better to wait and see if the mature elms that have survived continue to flourish.
questions.analysis:
第一步：拆解题干关键词。题干核心是“等待观察幸存成熟榆树是否继续繁荣”，需定位到主张“等待”“不干预”“依赖自然恢复”的人物观点。
 第二步：原文定位。在文章G段，Matt Eliot提到Woodland Trust的策略是“providing space to those elms that have survived independently”（为独立幸存的榆树提供空间），随后总结观点“Sometimes the best thing you can do is just give nature time to recover ... over time, you might get resistance”。
 第三步：线索词对应。“It may be better”对应“the best thing you can do is”，“to wait and see”对应“give nature time to recover”，“the mature elms that have survived”对应“those elms that have survived independently”，“continue to flourish”对应“resistance”（有抗性才能繁荣），逻辑完全匹配。
 第四步：确定答案句。Eliot的这句话直接表达了“等待自然恢复，让幸存榆树发展抗性以继续存活”的观点，与题干一致。

questions.title: There must be an explanation for the survival of some mature elms.
questions.analysis:
1. **题目定位**：题干核心是“一些成熟榆树的幸存背后一定有解释”，需要在原文中找到某个人物表达了“幸存并非偶然，必有原因”的观点。
 2. **关键词对应**： 
 - 题干“an explanation”（解释）对应原文“the reasons”（原因），均指向对“幸存”的归因； 
 - 题干“for the survival”（关于幸存）对应原文“for their survival”（关于它们的幸存），直接匹配核心事件； 
 - 题干“of some mature elms”（一些成熟榆树的）对应原文“their”，而“their”指代上文提到的“mature specimens”（成熟样本），即“一些成熟榆树”； 
 - 题干“There must be”（一定有）对应原文“I don't see how it can be entirely down to luck”（我不认为这完全是靠运气），逻辑上均否定“偶然因素”，强调“必然存在原因”。
 3. **答案句理解**：Karen Russell明确提问“它们存活的原因是什么？”，并否定“完全靠运气”，直接表明“幸存背后一定有解释（如回避、耐受性或抵抗力）”，与题干观点完全一致。

questions.title: We need to be aware that insects carrying Dutch elm disease are not very far away.
questions.analysis:
第一步：明确题目类型与题干核心。本题为人名观点匹配题，需将题干“我们必须意识到携带荷兰榆树病的昆虫离得不远”与文中人物观点对应。题干核心信息为“携带疾病的昆虫”“离得不远”“需警惕”。
 第二步：定位文中人名及对应内容。文章出现的人名有A. Matt Eliot、B. Karen Russell、C. Peter Bourne。需依次查看三人观点：
 - Matt Eliot：A段提及荷兰榆树病的影响，G段讨论重新引入榆树的担忧（如用园艺类似物替代原生种、克隆风险、新疾病风险），未涉及“昆虫距离近”。
 - Karen Russell：C段研究幸存榆树原因，F段测试杂交榆树抗病性，未提及“昆虫距离近”。
 - Peter Bourne：E段提到布莱顿市榆树因海风幸存，但“情况岌岌可危”，并指出“the beetles can just march in if we're not careful, as the threat is right on our doorstep”，直接涉及“昆虫（甲虫）”“威胁近（家门口）”“需警惕（不小心会入侵）”。
 第三步：匹配线索词确认答案。题干“insects carrying Dutch elm disease”对应文中“the beetles”（B段明确“elm bark beetles spread the deadly fungus”，即携带疾病的甲虫）；“We need to be aware”对应“if we're not careful”（均强调需警惕）；“are not very far away”对应“the threat is right on our doorstep”（“家门口”即“离得不远”）。因此答案句为Peter Bourne的观点。

questions.title: You understand the effect Dutch elm disease has had when you see evidence of how prominent the tree once was.
questions.analysis:
第一步：定位题干核心信息。题干的意思是“当你看到榆树曾经多么显赫的证据时，你就能理解荷兰榆树病带来的影响”，关键在于“理解疾病影响”和“看到树曾经显赫的证据”之间的关联。
 第二步：寻找文章中相关人名的观点。题目给出的选项是Matt Eliot（A）、Karen Russell（B）、Peter Bourne（C），需要在文章中找到这三个人名，并阅读他们的言论。
 第三步：匹配线索词。逐一对比题干线索词与各人名观点中的表述：
 - Matt Eliot在Section A提到“look at old photographs”（对应“you see evidence”）、“only then that you realise the impact”（对应“You understand the effect”）、“They were significant, large trees”（对应“how prominent the tree once was”），所有线索词均能对应。
 - Karen Russell的观点集中在研究幸存榆树原因（C段）、测试新品种抗病性（F段）、重新引种的社会接受度（G段），未提及“通过看过去证据理解疾病影响”。
 - Peter Bourne的观点是儿时目睹疾病（E段）、布莱顿榆树因海风幸存（E段），也未涉及题干核心逻辑。
 第四步：确定答案句。Matt Eliot的话完整匹配题干的逻辑关系，因此答案为A。

groups.title: Uses of a popular tree
groups.desc:
Questions 24-26
Complete the summary below.
Choose ONE WORD ONLY from the passage for each answer.
groups.collect: For hundreds of years, the only tree that was more popular in Britain than elm was $40040$. Starting in the Bronze Age, many tools were made from elm and people also used it to make weapons. In the 18th century, it was grown to provide wood for boxes and $40041$. Due to its strength, elm was often used for mining equipment and the Cutty Sark’s $40042$ was also constructed from elm.

questions.analysis:
1. **题目定位**：题干询问“在英国唯一比榆树更受欢迎的树”，核心线索为“For hundreds of years”“more popular in Britain than elm”。通过扫描文章，在Section D首句发现关键信息，其中“For centuries”对应“For hundreds of years”，“in Britain”原词重现，“elm ran a close second to oak”直接体现“橡树比榆树更受欢迎”的逻辑。
 2. **寻找答案句**：根据上述线索，确定Section D首句为答案句，该句明确对比了榆树和橡树的受欢迎程度。
 3. **答案句理解**：“elm ran a close second to oak”意为“榆树紧随橡树之后，位居第二”，即橡树（oak）是排名第一、比榆树更受欢迎的树，因此题干空白处应填“oak”。

central_sentences.sentence: For centuries, elm ran a close second to oak as the hardwood tree of choice in Britain and was in many instances the most prominent tree in the landscape.
central_sentences.translation: 几个世纪以来，榆树作为英国首选的硬木树种紧随橡树之后，位居第二，并且在许多情况下是景观中最显眼的树。

paragraphs.label: A
paragraphs.text: Some of the most important decisions of our lives occur while we’re feeling stressed and anxious. From medical decisions to financial and professional ones, we are all sometimes required to weigh up information under stressful conditions. But do we become better or worse at processing and using information under such circumstances?

paragraphs.label: B
paragraphs.text: My colleague and I, both neuroscientists, wanted to investigate how the mind operates under stress, so we visited some local fire stations. Firefighters’ workdays vary quite a bit. Some are pretty relaxed; they’ll spend their time washing the truck, cleaning equipment, cooking meals and reading. Other days can be hectic, with numerous life-threatening incidents to attend to; they’ll enter burning homes to rescue trapped residents, and assist with medical emergencies. These ups and downs presented the perfect setting for an experiment on how people’s ability to use information changes when they feel under pressure.

paragraphs.label: C
paragraphs.text: We found that perceived threat acted as a trigger for a stress reaction that made the task of processing information easier for the firefighters – but only as long as it conveyed bad news.

paragraphs.label: D
paragraphs.text: This is how we arrived at these results. We asked the firefighters to estimate their likelihood of experiencing 40 different adverse events in their life, such as being involved in an accident or becoming a victim of card fraud. We then gave them either good news (that their likelihood of experiencing these events was lower than they’d thought) or bad news (that it was higher) and asked them to provide new estimates.

paragraphs.label: E
paragraphs.text: People are normally quite optimistic – they will ignore bad news and embrace the good. This is what happened when the firefighters were relaxed; but when they were under stress, a different pattern emerged. Under these conditions, they became hyper-vigilant to bad news, even when it had nothing to do with their job (such as learning that the likelihood of card fraud was higher than they’d thought), and altered their beliefs in response. In contrast, stress didn’t change how they responded to good news (such as learning that the likelihood of card fraud was lower than they’d thought).

paragraphs.label: F
paragraphs.text: Back in our lab, we observed the same pattern in students who were told they had to give a surprise public speech, which would be judged by a panel, recorded and posted online. Sure enough, their cortisol levels spiked, their heart rates went up and they suddenly became better at processing unrelated, yet alarming, information about rates of disease and violence.

paragraphs.label: G
paragraphs.text: When we experience stressful events, a physiological change is triggered that causes us to take in warnings and focus on what might go wrong. Brain imaging reveals that this ‘switch’ is related to a sudden boost in a neural signal important for learning, specifically in response to unexpected warning signs, such as faces expressing fear.

paragraphs.label: H
paragraphs.text: Such neural engineering could have helped prehistoric humans to survive. When our ancestors found themselves surrounded by hungry animals, they would have benefited from an increased ability to learn about hazards. In a safe environment, however, it would have been wasteful to be on high alert constantly. So, a neural switch that automatically increases or decreases our ability to process warnings in response to changes in our environment could have been useful. In fact, people with clinical depression and anxiety seem unable to switch away from a state in which they absorb all the negative messages around them.

paragraphs.label: I
paragraphs.text: It is also important to realise that stress travels rapidly from one person to the next. If a co-worker is stressed, we are more likely to tense up and feel stressed ourselves. We don’t even need to be in the same room with someone for their emotions to influence our behaviour. Studies show that if we observe positive feeds on social media, such as images of a pink sunset, we are more likely to post uplifting messages ourselves. If we observe negative posts, such as complaints about a long queue at the coffee shop, we will in turn create more negative posts.

paragraphs.label: J
paragraphs.text: In some ways, many of us now live as if we are in danger, constantly ready to tackle demanding emails and text messages, and respond to news alerts and comments on social media. Repeatedly checking your phone, according to a survey conducted by the American Psychological Association, is related to stress. In other words, a pre-programmed physiological reaction, which evolution has equipped us with to help us avoid famished predators, is now being triggered by an online post. Social media posting, according to one study, raises your pulse, makes you sweat, and enlarges your pupils more than most daily activities.

paragraphs.label: K
paragraphs.text: The fact that stress increases the likelihood that we will focus more on alarming messages, together with the fact that it spreads extremely rapidly, can create collective fear that is not always justified. After a stressful public event, such as a natural disaster or major financial crash, there is often a wave of alarming information in traditional and social media, which individuals become very aware of. But that has the effect of exaggerating existing danger. And so, a reliable pattern emerges – stress is triggered, spreading from one person to the next, which temporarily enhances the likelihood that people will take in negative reports, which increases stress further. As a result, trips are cancelled, even if the disaster took place across the globe; stocks are sold, even when holding on is the best thing to do.

paragraphs.label: L
paragraphs.text: The good news, however, is that positive emotions, such as hope, are contagious too, and are powerful in inducing people to act to find solutions. Being aware of the close relationship between people’s emotional state and how they process information can help us frame our messages more effectively and become conscientious agents of change.

Questions 27-30
Choose the correct letter, A, B, C or D.

questions.title: In the first paragraph, the writer introduces the topic of the text by

options.text: mentioning a challenge faced by everyone.
specifying a situation which makes us most anxious.

questions.analysis: 题目问的是作者在第一段如何引入文章主题。首先，我们看题干，核心是“introduces the topic by”。然后我们看选项C是“mentioning a challenge faced by everyone”。我们去原文找对应的内容。原文开头说生活中一些重要决定是在有压力和焦虑时做出的，接着具体举例“From medical decisions to financial and professional ones”，然后关键句来了“We are all sometimes required to weigh up information under stressful conditions”。这里“we are all”就对应了选项C中的“faced by everyone”，“weigh up information under stressful conditions”（在压力下权衡信息）这明显是一种“a challenge”（一个挑战）。所以作者就是通过提及一个每个人都会面临的挑战来引入主题的。

central_sentences.sentence: From medical decisions to financial and professional ones, we are all sometimes required to weigh up information under stressful conditions.
central_sentences.translation: 从医疗决策到财务和职业决策，我们所有人有时都需要在充满压力的情况下权衡信息。

questions.title: What point does the writer make about firefighters in the second paragraph?

options.text: The regular changes of stress levels in their working lives make them ideal study subjects.
The strategies they use to handle stress are of particular interest to researchers.
The stressful nature of their job is typical of many public service professions.
Their personalities make them especially well-suited to working under stress.

questions.analysis: 首先，题目问的是作者在第二段中关于消防员提出了什么观点。我们需要从第二段中找到作者的核心观点句。通过阅读第二段，我们发现最后一句“These ups and downs presented the perfect setting for an experiment on how people’s ability to use information changes when they feel under pressure.”是总结性的句子，很可能就是答案句。接着，我们将选项A与这句话及上下文进行比对。“regular changes”对应原文的“ups and downs”，描述了消防员工作状态的变化；“stress levels”对应“under stress”和“under pressure”，点明了研究的核心是压力；“in their working lives”对应“Firefighters’ workdays”，说明这种变化发生在工作中；“make them ideal”对应“presented the perfect setting”，表明这种情况对实验来说是理想的；“study subjects”对应“for an experiment”，因为实验需要研究对象，消防员自然就是这个实验的研究对象。综合这些线索词的对应关系，可以确定答案句直接支持了选项A的观点。

central_sentences.sentence: These ups and downs presented the perfect setting for an experiment on how people’s ability to use information changes when they feel under pressure.
central_sentences.translation: 这些起伏为我们提供了一个完美的环境，来研究人们在感到压力时运用信息的能力是如何变化的。

questions.title: What is the writer doing in the fourth paragraph?
questions.analysis:
1. 题目定位：题目询问作者在第四段的行为，需要理解段落主旨。
2. 寻找关键词：正确答案D选项中的核心词是"describing methodology"（描述方法论）。
3. 定位答案句：整个第四段均为答案句，首句"This is how we arrived at these results"（这就是我们得出这些结果的方法）直接点明下文将介绍方法，随后详细描述了研究步骤。
4. 理解答案句：段落通过"We asked...We then gave...and asked..."等一系列动作描述，完整呈现了研究的具体操作流程，即"methodology"（方法论）。"how we arrived at these results"与"methodology"直接对应，"This is how...We asked...We then gave..."的叙述方式对应"describing"。

central_sentences.sentence: This is how we arrived at these results. We asked the firefighters to estimate their likelihood of experiencing 40 different adverse events in their life, such as being involved in an accident or becoming a victim of card fraud. We then gave them either good news (that their likelihood of experiencing these events was lower than they’d thought) or bad news (that it was higher) and asked them to provide new estimates.
central_sentences.translation: 这就是我们得出这些结果的方法。我们要求消防员们估计他们在生活中经历40种不同不良事件的可能性，例如卷入事故或成为信用卡诈骗的受害者。然后，我们给他们要么是好消息（即他们经历这些事件的可能性比他们想象的要低），要么是坏消息（即可能性比他们想象的要高），并要求他们提供新的估计。

questions.title: In the seventh paragraph, the writer describes a mechanism in the brain which

options.text: enables people to respond more quickly to stressful situations.
results in increased ability to control our levels of anxiety.
produces heightened sensitivity to indications of external threats.
is activated when there is a need to communicate a sense of danger.

questions.analysis: 首先，题干问的是作者描述的大脑机制的作用。我们通过题干中的“mechanism in the brain”定位到原文的“this ‘switch’”。然后找到关键的答案句：“When we experience stressful events, a physiological change is triggered that causes us to take in warnings and focus on what might go wrong.”以及补充说明句“...specifically in response to unexpected warning signs, such as faces expressing fear.”。答案句表明这个机制会让我们接收警告、关注可能出错的事，并且对意外警告信号有反应。这其实就是说我们对外部那些可能是威胁的迹象变得更敏感了，和选项C的意思完全相符。

central_sentences.translation: 当我们经历压力事件时，一种生理变化被触发，这使我们接收警告并关注可能出错的事情。

central_sentences.sentence: Brain imaging reveals that this ‘switch’ is related to a sudden boost in a neural signal important for learning, specifically in response to unexpected warning signs, such as faces expressing fear.

questions.title: The students' cortisol levels and heart rates were affected when the researchers
questions.analysis:
1. **定位题目关键词**：题目中的关键信息是 "The students' cortisol levels and heart rates were affected when the researchers"。我们需要找到学生的皮质醇水平和心率在什么情况下受到了影响，也就是研究人员做了什么。
 2. **定位原文段落**：带着关键词 "students", "cortisol levels", "heart rates" 浏览文章，可以很快定位到文章的第六段。
 3. **找到答案句**：第六段明确提到："Back in our lab, we observed the same pattern in students who were told they had to give a surprise public speech, which would be judged by a panel, recorded and posted online. Sure enough, their cortisol levels spiked, their heart rates went up..."。
 4. **理解答案句**：这句话的前半部分 "students who were told they had to give a surprise public speech..." 描述了研究人员对学生所做的事情——告知他们需要做一个令人紧张的公开演讲（这显然是一个压力情境）。后半部分 "their cortisol levels spiked, their heart rates went up" 正是题干中提到的 "cortisol levels and heart rates were affected"。
 5. **匹配选项**：题目问的是 "when the researchers"（当研究人员做了什么时）。原文中 "students who were told..." 中的 "told" 这个动作的发出者就是研究人员（"we"）。研究人员告诉学生要做演讲，这相当于 "put them in a stressful situation"（把他们置于一个压力情境中）。因此，选项 E 是正确的。

central_sentences.sentence: Back in our lab, we observed the same pattern in students who were told they had to give a surprise public speech, which would be judged by a panel, recorded and posted online. Sure enough, their cortisol levels spiked, their heart rates went up and they suddenly became better at processing unrelated, yet alarming, information about rates of disease and violence.
central_sentences.translation: 回到我们的实验室，我们在学生身上观察到了同样的模式：我们告诉这些学生，他们必须做一个 surprise 的公开演讲，这个演讲会由一个评审团评判、录制并发布到网上。果然，他们的皮质醇水平飙升，心率加快，并且突然变得更善于处理与疾病和暴力发生率相关的、不相关但令人担忧的信息。

questions.analysis:
第一步，定位题干关键词。题干核心是“在两个实验中，当受试者处于何种状态时，负面信息会被更好地处理”，需要找到与“两个实验”“负面信息”“处理更好”“受试者状态”相关的原文内容。
 第二步，在原文中寻找对应实验描述。文章提到了两个实验：一是消防员实验，二是学生实验。消防员实验中提到“当他们处于压力下时，对坏消息变得高度警惕”；学生实验中提到“处于压力情境（准备演讲）时，更擅长处理令人警惕的信息”。
 第三步，确定答案句。消防员实验中“when they were under stress... they became hyper-vigilant to bad news”和学生实验中“their cortisol levels spiked... became better at processing... alarming information”直接说明，压力状态下负面信息处理更好。
 第四步，理解逻辑关系。两个实验共同表明，“压力”是“负面信息处理更好”的前提条件，题干问“when the subjects”（当受试者...时），即需要描述受试者的状态，“were feeling under stress”（感到有压力）准确对应这一状态。

central_sentences.sentence: We found that perceived threat acted as a trigger for a stress reaction that made the task of processing information easier for the firefighters – but only as long as it conveyed bad news.
central_sentences.translation: 我们发现，感知到的威胁会触发压力反应，这使得消防员处理信息的任务变得更容易——但前提是这些信息传递的是坏消息。

central_sentences.sentence: This is what happened when the firefighters were relaxed; but when they were under stress, a different pattern emerged. Under these conditions, they became hyper-vigilant to bad news, even when it had nothing to do with their job (such as learning that the likelihood of card fraud was higher than they’d thought), and altered their beliefs in response.
central_sentences.translation: 这就是消防员放松时的情况；但当他们处于压力下时，一种不同的模式出现了。在这种情况下，他们对坏消息变得高度警惕，即使这些消息与他们的工作无关（例如得知信用卡诈骗的可能性比他们想象的要高），并会相应地改变自己的信念。

central_sentences.sentence: Back in our lab, we observed the same pattern in students who were told they had to give a surprise public speech, which would be judged by a panel, recorded and posted online. Sure enough, their cortisol levels spiked, their heart rates went up and they suddenly became better at processing unrelated, yet alarming, information about rates of disease and violence.
central_sentences.translation: 回到实验室，我们在学生身上观察到了同样的模式：我们告诉学生他们必须进行一场意外的公开演讲，演讲会由评审团评判、录制并发布到网上。果然，他们的皮质醇水平飙升，心率加快，并且突然变得更擅长处理与疾病和暴力发生率相关的、无关但令人警惕的信息。

questions.title: The tone of the content we post on social media tends to reflect the nature of the posts in our feeds.
questions.analysis: 首先，理解题干意思：我们在社交媒体上发布内容的基调往往会反映我们信息流中帖子的性质。接着，带着题干中的关键信息，如"The tone of the content we post"、"tends to reflect"、"the nature of the posts"、"in our feeds"去原文中寻找。很快定位到文章第九段的相关句子。然后分析答案句，该句通过两个并列的假设情况说明，当看到积极的信息流时，我们更可能发布积极内容；看到负面帖子时，会发布更多负面内容。这与题干中“发布内容的基调反映信息流帖子性质”的表述完全一致，所以题干信息可以在文章中找到。

central_sentences.sentence: Studies show that if we observe positive feeds on social media, such as images of a pink sunset, we are more likely to post uplifting messages ourselves. If we observe negative posts, such as complaints about a long queue at the coffee shop, we will in turn create more negative posts.
central_sentences.translation: 研究表明，如果我们在社交媒体上看到积极的信息流，例如粉色日落的图片，我们自己更有可能发布令人振奋的消息。如果我们看到负面的帖子，例如抱怨咖啡店排起长队，我们反过来也会发布更多负面的帖子。

questions.title: Phones have a greater impact on our stress levels than other electronic media devices.
questions.analysis:
第一步，题目定位与关键词识别。题干核心信息为“Phones have a greater impact on our stress levels than other electronic media devices.”，需要找到手机对压力水平的影响是否比其他电子媒体设备更大的相关内容。关键线索词包括“Phones”、“have a greater impact on”、“our stress levels”、“greater”、“than”、“other electronic media devices”。
第二步，根据关键词在原文中搜索。在文章第十段找到与“Phones”和“stress”相关的句子“Repeatedly checking your phone, according to a survey conducted by the American Psychological Association, is related to stress.”，其中“Phones”对应“phone”，“have a greater impact on”对应“is related to”，“our stress levels”对应“stress”。还找到“Social media posting, according to one study, raises your pulse, makes you sweat, and enlarges your pupils more than most daily activities.”，提到了社交媒体发帖的生理反应，但“social media”不等同于“other electronic media devices”。
第三步，分析答案句含义。第一句表明反复查看手机与压力有关，但未涉及与其他电子媒体设备的比较。第二句提到社交媒体发帖比大多数日常活动有更强烈生理反应，但“大多数日常活动”并非“其他电子媒体设备”，且未提及手机与其他电子媒体设备对压力影响的比较。题干中的“greater than other electronic media devices”这一比较关系在原文中没有任何线索词对应，原文未提及“other electronic media devices”，也没有对手机和其他电子媒体设备的影响进行比较。

central_sentences.translation: 根据美国心理协会的一项调查，反复查看手机与压力有关。

central_sentences.translation: 根据一项研究，社交媒体发帖比大多数日常活动更能提高脉搏、让人出汗、扩大瞳孔。

questions.title: The more we read about a stressful public event on social media, the less able we are to take the information in.
questions.analysis: 首先，我们通过题干中的关键信息“a stressful public event”和“on social media”定位到文章倒数第二段。在这一段中，我们找到了与题干相关的答案句。这些句子表明，在经历有压力的公共事件后，社交媒体上会出现大量令人担忧的信息。关键在于，原文提到“stress increases the likelihood that we will focus more on alarming messages”（压力会增加我们关注警报信息的可能性）、“individuals become very aware of”（人们会非常清楚地意识到这些信息）以及“temporarily enhances the likelihood that people will take in negative reports”（暂时增强了人们吸收负面报道的可能性）。这与题干中“the less able we are to take the information in”（我们越不能吸收这些信息）的表述完全相反。题干说的是阅读越多压力事件信息，吸收能力越弱，而原文明确指出压力会让人更关注、更能意识到并吸收负面信息。因此，题干的信息与原文信息相反。

central_sentences.sentence: The fact that stress increases the likelihood that we will focus more on alarming messages, together with the fact that it spreads extremely rapidly, can create collective fear that is not always justified.
central_sentences.translation: 压力会增加我们关注警报信息的可能性，再加上压力传播得极快，这可能会造成并不总是合理的集体恐慌。

central_sentences.sentence: After a stressful public event, such as a natural disaster or major financial crash, there is often a wave of alarming information in traditional and social media, which individuals become very aware of.
central_sentences.translation: 在发生有压力的公共事件之后，比如自然灾害或重大金融危机，传统媒体和社交媒体上往往会出现一波令人担忧的信息，人们会非常清楚地意识到这些信息。

central_sentences.sentence: But that has the effect of exaggerating existing danger.
central_sentences.translation: 但这会产生夸大现有危险的效果。

central_sentences.sentence: And so, a reliable pattern emerges – stress is triggered, spreading from one person to the next, which temporarily enhances the likelihood that people will take in negative reports, which increases stress further.
central_sentences.translation: 于是，一种可靠的模式出现了——压力被触发，从一个人传播到另一个人，这暂时增强了人们吸收负面报道的可能性，进而进一步增加了压力。

questions.title: Stress created by social media posts can lead us to take unnecessary precautions.
questions.analysis:
第一步：定位题干关键词。题干核心为“Stress created by social media posts can lead us to take unnecessary precautions”，需拆解为“Stress created by social media posts”“can lead us to take”“unnecessary precautions”三个关键部分。
 第二步：匹配原文线索词。在原文中，“social media posts”对应“online post”（在线帖子即社交媒体帖子），“created by”对应“triggered by”（触发即产生）；“can lead us to take”对应“can create ... As a result”（造成……结果是）；“unnecessary precautions”对应“collective fear that is not always justified”（不总是合理的恐惧即不必要的）及具体行为例子“trips are cancelled... stocks are sold...”（取消旅行、抛售股票是典型的预防措施）。
 第三步：整合答案句逻辑。原文先指出社交媒体帖子（online post）会触发压力反应（pre-programmed physiological reaction），接着说明压力会引发“不总是合理的集体恐惧”，最终导致“取消远方旅行、抛售不该卖的股票”等行为，这些行为正是“不必要的预防措施”，完整对应题干逻辑。

central_sentences.sentence: In other words, a pre-programmed physiological reaction, which evolution has equipped us with to help us avoid famished predators, is now being triggered by an online post.
central_sentences.translation: 换句话说，一种进化赋予我们用来躲避饥饿捕食者的、预设的生理反应，现在正被一个在线帖子所触发。

central_sentences.sentence: The fact that stress increases the likelihood that we will focus more on alarming messages, together with the fact that it spreads extremely rapidly, can create collective fear that is not always justified.
central_sentences.translation: 压力会增加我们更关注警示性信息的可能性，再加上压力传播得极快这一事实，可能会造成并不总是合理的集体恐惧。

central_sentences.sentence: As a result, trips are cancelled, even if the disaster took place across the globe; stocks are sold, even when holding on is the best thing to do.
central_sentences.translation: 结果就是，旅行被取消，即使灾难远在地球另一端发生；股票被抛售，即使继续持有才是最佳选择。

questions.title: Our tendency to be affected by other people's moods can be used in a positive way.
questions.analysis: 首先，题目询问“我们容易受他人情绪影响的倾向，可以被积极地利用”这一信息是否能在文中找到。我们需要在原文中定位与“受他人情绪影响”、“积极利用”相关的内容。通过寻找线索词，发现“contagious”（有感染力的）对应“tendency to be affected by other people's moods”，“positive emotions”对应积极的情绪影响。“can help us frame our messages more effectively”和“become conscientious agents of change”则体现了“can be used”。而“The good news”、“positive emotions”、“powerful in inducing people to act to find solutions”等短语明确了“in a positive way”。在文章最后一段找到了关键的答案句，这些句子指出积极情绪也具有传染性，能引导人们寻找解决方案，并且意识到情绪与信息处理的关系有助于更有效地组织信息和推动变革。综合这些线索和答案句，可以判断题干信息在文中存在。

central_sentences.sentence: The good news, however, is that positive emotions, such as hope, are contagious too, and are powerful in inducing people to act to find solutions.
central_sentences.translation: 然而，好消息是，积极情绪，比如希望，也具有传染性，并且能有力地引导人们采取行动寻找解决方案。

central_sentences.sentence: Being aware of the close relationship between people’s emotional state and how they process information can help us frame our messages more effectively and become conscientious agents of change.
central_sentences.translation: 意识到人们的情绪状态和他们处理信息方式之间的密切关系，能帮助我们更有效地组织信息，并成为尽责的变革推动者。
